coredata.dc:title: In-situ direct grafting of graphene quantum dots onto carbon fibre by low temperature chemical synthesis for high performance flexible fabric supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Islam, Mohammad S.
Deng, Yan
Tong, Liyong
Roy, Anup K.
Faisal, Shaikh N.
Hassan, Mahbub
Minett, Andrew I.
Gomes, Vincent G.
coredata.dc:description: A novel chemical synthesis method has been developed to directly graft graphene quantum dots (GQDs) onto carbon fibre (CF) in-situ via covalent ester linkages at low temperature (90°C). SEM and TEM images depict the grafting of GQDs on CF showing about 75% of the GQD falls below 40nm. The grafting was further confirmed by FTIR, RAMAN, XPS and XRD spectroscopy. The flexible fabric supercapacitor manufactured using the grafted materials shows increased electrochemical capacitance (about 5.5 times) than that of CF by providing active surface sites of GQDs, creating defects on the surface and preventing GQD recombination. This supercapacitor maintained approximately 97% of its initial capacitance after 5000 cycles at 2Ag−1 current density, suggesting a stable performance of the supercapacitor and holds a promising potential in the field of advanced flexible electronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Carbon fibre and fabric
Flexible supercapacitors
Grafting
Chemical synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Desizing of CFs and oxidation of CF and graphene
Reduction of carboxylic functionalised graphene to hydroxyl functionalised graphene
Low temperature direct grafting of graphene quantum dots (GQD) on CF
Production of flexible supercapacitor and measurement of supercapacitive performance
In-situ synthesis of GQDs and their grafting on CF
Measurement of supercapacitive performances of CF and GQD-CF grafted structure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the complete grafting process including oxidation of graphene sheets, reduction of oxidised graphene and grafting of GQD on CF via ester linkage.

(a) A 1μ scale SEM image of GQD grafted on CF and (b) A 100nm scale SEM image of GQD grafted on CF with particle size distribution shown as inset; (c) a 20nm scale TEM image of GQDs and (d) the size distribution histogram of TEM images.

(a) FTIR spectra of CF, GQD and GQDs grafted on CF, (b) Raman spectra of CF, GQD and GQDs grafted on CF, (c) XPS wide scan spectra of GQDs grafted on CF, (d) Curve fitting of C 1s peak area to surface functional groups of GQDs grafted on CF, and (e) Curve fitting of O 1s peak area to surface functional groups of GQDs grafted on CF.

(a) A two electrode capacitor device, (b) Woven cloth type structure of GQDs-CF showing its flexibility, (c) A comparison of cyclic voltammetry for CF and GQDs grafted on CF at 100mVs−1, (d) Cyclic voltammetry of GQDs grafted on CF at 20–100mVs−1 scan rates, (e) Electrochemical charge-discharge curves at 1Ag−1 for CF and GQDs grafted on CF, (f) Charge-discharge curves of GQDs grafted on CF at 1−10Ag−1.

(a) Schematic of sandwich-type two electrode GQDs-CF flexible supercapacitor, (b) Charge-discharge of the device with and without bending and (c) Percentage capacitance retention versus cycle number curve of GQDs-CF flexible supercapacitor.

head.ce:title: In-situ direct grafting of graphene quantum dots onto carbon fibre by low temperature chemical synthesis for high performance flexible fabric supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Aerospace, Mechanical and Mechatronic Engineering, The University of Sydney, Sydney, NSW 2006, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Sustainable Technology, School of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, The University of Sydney, Sydney, NSW 2006, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Integrated Polymer and Systems Engineering Group, School of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, The University of Sydney, Sydney, NSW 2006, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Integrated Polymer and Systems Engineering Group
School of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel chemical synthesis method has been developed to directly graft graphene quantum dots (GQDs) onto carbon fibre (CF) in-situ via covalent ester linkages at low temperature (90°C). SEM and TEM images depict the grafting of GQDs on CF showing about 75% of the GQD falls below 40nm. The grafting was further confirmed by FTIR, RAMAN, XPS and XRD spectroscopy. The flexible fabric supercapacitor manufactured using the grafted materials shows increased electrochemical capacitance (about 5.5 times) than that of CF by providing active surface sites of GQDs, creating defects on the surface and preventing GQD recombination. This supercapacitor maintained approximately 97% of its initial capacitance after 5000 cycles at 2Ag−1 current density, suggesting a stable performance of the supercapacitor and holds a promising potential in the field of advanced flexible electronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted extensive attention from scientists due to their excellent properties of edge effect, high specific surface area and mobility, strong luminescence properties, while keeping good electrical conductivity, biocompatibility and dispersion ability in various solvents. Based on these advantages, GQDs have exhibited promise in the field of current and next generation of supercapacitors, bio-imaging, photovoltaics, light-emitting diodes, environmental sensors, and fuel cells [1–6].

Compared with traditional rechargeable batteries, electrochemical supercapacitors show enhanced power density, faster charge-discharge rates and longer cycle life, due to their unique energy storage mechanism. In addition, the introduction of nanomaterials makes supercapacitors achieve acceptable energy density close to that of batteries [7,8]. All these advantages place supercapacitors as potential supplement to batteries in energy storage and harvesting applications [2]. The development of lightweight, flexible supercapacitors with high capacitance performances even under mechanical deformation is of particular interest [9]. Thus far, activated carbon fibre cloth [10] and carbon nanotube-graphene hybrid fibres [11] have been attempted as flexible electrochemical capacitors. On the other hand, a nanocomposite of poly(pyrrole) and GQD has been obtained through in-situ polymerization of pyrrole in the presence of GQD suspension and used as electrode material [12]. The capacitive properties of the as-formed GQD/CNT hybrid arrays have also been evaluated in symmetrical supercapacitors [13].

The use of GQDs grafted onto CF network as a flexible electrode material for supercapacitors is yet to be explored. The main drawback of high recombination rate of GQDs is expected to be reduced by grafting them onto CF and thus the efficiency and durability of the as-made supercapacitors will be improved. Consequently, we explored the use of GQDs grafted on CF for high-performance flexible supercapacitors in the current study. To achieve this, a GQD-CF//GQD-CF symmetric supercapacitor is prepared using a sandwich-type arrangement of pressed GQD-CF woven cloth-like structure, followed by the characterization of its electrochemical properties in aqueous electrolyte media. The results show that the synthesized GQDs#CF-based supercapacitors display superior electrochemical properties, including exceptionally high rate capability and high frequency response. The encouraging results make GQDs grafted on CF a promising candidate for the next generation of high-performance supercapacitors.

Toray's T300 standard modulus carbon fibre tow (12k x 60m long) with Young’s modulus of 230GPa and tensile strength of 3530MPa from Carbon Fibre Australia are used in this study. The graphene used in the current study is the few layer graphene sheets that are synthesized as per the work reported previously [14]. According to the method, 20mL distilled water is mixed with 0.1wt.% expanded graphite along with some sodium dodecyl sulfate surfactant to obtain a dispersion. The dispersion is then sonicated at room temperature for 60–80min by placing in an ice bath to counter any temperature rise during the process using a tip horn sonicator (Branson 450D) with a cylindrical tip with 10mm end cap diameter. The output power of the sonicator is 30W of delivered power at 1650–1800Jmin−1 level. To obtain 1–10 layer graphene sheets approximately 90wt.% graphene is separated by centrifugation and sedimentation. Analytical grade nitric acid (HNO3) and H2SO4, borane (BH3) in tetrahydrofuran (THF) and dimethyl formamide (DMF) from Sigma-Aldrich are used for functionalization and grafting work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Desizing of CFs and oxidation of CF and graphene
ce:section.ce:para: 50mg CF is desized with acetone by refluxing at 60°C for 48h and then dried overnight at 65°C. For oxidizing graphene and CF separately, 100mL of 1:1 (v/v) mixture of 70% HNO3 and deionised water is taken in a round bottom flask with 50mg preweighed CFs/graphene to sonicate at room temperature for 30min. The mixture is then refluxed at 60°C for 6h. After cooling to room temperature, each of the reaction mixtures of CF and graphene is filtrated using vacuum and washed several times with deionized water to obtain a pH of 7 and then dried overnight at 65°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reduction of carboxylic functionalised graphene to hydroxyl functionalised graphene
ce:section.ce:para: 10mg of oxidised graphene was mixed with 5mL BH3 in THF at 0 to −5°C for 10minutes. The mixture was then warmed to 25°C and left for 48h [15]. The reduced graphene (hydroxyl functionalised) was then vacuum-filtrated and dried overnight at 65°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Low temperature direct grafting of graphene quantum dots (GQD) on CF
ce:section.ce:para: 2mg of carboxyl functionalised CF is separated from each other and placed in a vial. A couple of drops of 98% H2SO4 are added to the mixture. 2mg of hydroxyl functionalised graphene is mixed with 10mL of DMF in a glass vial to sonicate the mixture at room temperature for 20min. The mixture is then heated to 90°C for 30min. After heating, the mixture is added to the CF kept in the vial and then refluxed at 90°C for 1h. 4Å molecular sieve is used to remove water produced during reaction. After cooling to room temperature, the GQDs grafted on CF are vacuum-filtrated and dried overnight at 65°C.

The morphology of the samples is examined by using a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Zeiss ULTRA plus, Germany). FTIR spectroscopy analysis has been carried out using a Bruker Tensor 27 FTIR Spectrometer with Hyperion 3000 FPA microscope. For each sample, 100 scans were recorded in absorbance unit from 4000 to 900cm−1. Spectra were obtained using attenuated total reflectance (ATR). Materials surfaces analyzed were in contact with a ZnSe crystal with a 45° angle of incidence. The Raman spectra are measured using a Renishaw microRaman spectroscopy system with a 514.5nm argon-ion laser under ambient conditions. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis is carried out using an instrument (ESCALAB250Xi) with mono-chromated Al K alpha (energy 1486.68eV) X-ray source of radiation at 90° electron take-off angle. The wide angle X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns are obtained by using a Philip X-ray diffractometer with a current of 40mA and a voltage of 40 mV with a CuKα irradiation source (λ=1.54A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production of flexible supercapacitor and measurement of supercapacitive performance
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the application of GQDs-CF in flexible and wearable electronic devices, textile electrodes were fabricated using GQDs grafted on CFs. Due to the rough surface of the individual GQDs-CF, it was difficult to directly knit GQDs-CF into textile manually in the laboratory. Therefore, the GQDs-CFs were pressed using a hydraulic press at 1MPa pressure into woven cloth like structure, which is highly flexible. A thin ITO coated polyethylene terephthalate (PET) film with a bending radius of 15mm is used as the supporting substrate and current collector. The separator is a filter paper soaked with H2SO4 gel electrolyte. The GQD-CF cloth is first soaked with aqueous electrolyte for wetting and then coated with the gel electrolyte.

The carbon fibres and the fibrous film of GQDs grafted on CF are directly used as working electrodes by clamping with clamp-type gold wire as current collector in a three-electrode cell with Pt wire as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge–discharge measurements are carried out using a Biologic SP300 digital electrochemical workstation with potential window from −0.0 to 0.8V in 1M H2SO4 electrolyte in the case of three electrode system and H2SO4 gel (H2SO4 dissolved in polyvinyl alcohol) in the case of two electrode system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ synthesis of GQDs and their grafting on CF
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows a schematic of the grafting process starting with oxidation of graphene sheets to form carboxyl groups followed by reduction of carboxyl groups to hydroxyl groups. The hydroxyl functionalised reduced graphene is then grafted with the carboxyl groups of CF via ester linkage. During sonication and oxidation reaction the graphene sheets are broken down to smaller pieces with mainly surface carboxyl groups on them which are further broken down to smaller fragments when carboxyl groups are converted to hydroxyl groups by sonication and subsequent reduction reaction with borane (BH3) in tetrahydrofuran (THF). Further sonication and grafting reaction of the reduced (hydroxyl functionalised) graphene with DMF, have produced GQDs. The mixture of GQDs (with significant amount of hydroxyl groups) is then added to carboxyl functionalised CFs in the presence of concentrated H2SO4 and molecular sieves to graft GQDs on CF in-situ via ester linkages. Grafting reaction has been carried out at a low temperature (90°C) and completed within 1h to obtain well-distributed GQDs on CF.

It is not unlikely that during grafting some of the GQDs can adsorb on CF through van der Waal’s force and therefore, grafted fibres were subjected to sonication for 1h at room temperature. Fig. S2 shows the SEM images of the grafted fibres before and after sonication. It can be seen from the images that the distribution and density of GQDs remain almost similar before and after sonication which could be due to the fact that the van der Waals' force is strong enough for structural stability.

The morphology of as received and treated CF (Fig. S1) as well as GQD-grafted CF is studied using a scanning electron microscope (SEM). Fig. 2 shows high-resolution SEM images with different magnifications, representing the covalent grafting of GQDs onto CF surface with a homogeneous distribution of GQDs all along. The size distribution histogram (inset of Fig. 2b) obtained from the SEM images depicts a size range of 1–100nm with approximately 75% falling below 40nm. More SEM images for GQDs grafted on CF taken at different magnification can be seen in Fig. S3.

Fig. 2(c) shows a TEM image of GQD obtained after isolation via dialysis. The GQD for this TEM image was taken from the solution immediately after grafting reaction was completed. The size distribution histogram shown in Fig. 2(d) obtained from the TEM images depicts a size range of 1–100nm with approximately 78% falling below 40nm which was very close to the results obtained using SEM images.

FTIR spectra of GQDs exhibit broadened peaks at 1725 and 1637cm−1 due to CO and CC bond vibrations. Another sharp peak is observed at 1405cm−1 for the COH stretching vibrations (Fig. 3 a). For the GQDs grafted on CF sample, oxygen containing groups including carboxyl, hydroxyl and ester groups are introduced as can be seen from the FTIR spectra (Fig. 3a). A strong peak at 3425cm−1 corresponds to the stretching mode of hydroxyl group (−OH) while the peak at 1724cm−1 indicates carbonyl stretching in carboxylic functional group (−COOH). The CC vibration of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms is detected at 1579cm−1 and a shoulder peak at 1054cm−1 is designated as CO stretching [16]. The peaks at 2854 and 2921cm−1 indicate sp3 carbon-hydrogen stretching and the peak at 1752cm−1 corresponds to carbonyl stretching [17–19]. These three main peaks indicate interaction between GQDs and CF and the formation of ester linkage between them.

From the Raman spectra shown in Fig. 3b, it can be seen that for CF, GQDs and GQDs grafted on CF samples, the signals corresponding to D bands occur at 1370, 1353 and 1348cm−1 and the signals corresponding to G bands occur at 1587, 1604 and 1596cm−1, respectively, which is possibly associated with disordered and graphitized carbon, respectively [20–22]. For GQDs grafted on CF sample, the G peak shifts towards higher wavelength compared with the CF sample and towards lower wavelength compared with GQD sample. For the GQD−grafted CF sample, the relatively high intensity of the D band than that of CF sample and relatively low intensity of the D band than that of GQD sample, indicates the presence of GQDs on CF [23]. The 2D peak of GQDs is seen at 2667cm−1 instead of 2700cm−1, indicating the interaction of GQDs with CF. The relative intensity of disorder D band to the intensity of crystalline G band (ID/IG) for GQDs grafted on CF sample is 0.92 which is significantly higher than the value (0.83) of CF sample, indicative of the bonding between GQDs and CF [24]. The ID/IG ratio obtained for the GQDs grafted on CF in the current study is higher than the values reported for GQDs prepared by chemical [24] and electrochemical routes [25]. The CF sample shows broad peaks with linewidths of approximately 200cm−1 (D band) and 152cm−1 (G band), indicating a rather low degree of ordering of the graphitic structures. The bands significantly narrow down to linewidth of 167cm−1 (D band) and 143cm−1 (G band) for the GQDs grafted on CF sample further indicating interaction between GQDs and CFs to graft GQDs on CF.

To determine any difference in chemical composition of GQDs grafted on CF samples, XPS C1s and O1s spectra have been measured (Fig. 3c-e). The peaks for carbon and oxygen are centred at 284.5eV and 531.5eV, respectively. It can be seen from Fig. 3c that GQDs grafted on CF sample contains 80 at.% carbon, 17 at.% oxygen and insignificant amount of nitrogen and chloride. The deconvolution of C1s peaks (Fig. 3d) at 284.5eV (peak 1) originates from the sp2 hybridized graphitic carbon [26] while the peak at 285.1eV (Peak 2) can be associated with sp2 hybridized carbon bonded to carbon with defects.23 We note that the “defect peak” with an energy shift of 0.2–1.5eV relative to the sp2 hybridized graphitic carbon bond is sometimes mistakenly considered as the CC (sp3) bond. This peak is usually located at 285.1eV and is often found in nanodiamond films and amorphous carbon [27]. The presence of other peaks at 286.5eV (peak 3), 288.3eV (peak 4) and 290.3 (peak 5), can be associated with CO (alcohol, ether, phenol), −COOH (carboxylic) groups and π−π*, respectively [1,28]. XPS wide scan spectra and curve fitting of C 1s peak area to surface functional groups of GQD and CF samples are given in Fig. S4 where sp2 hybridized CC bonding is greater for GQD sample compared to CF sample and sp3 hybridized CC bonding is greater for CF sample compared to GQD sample while GQD grafted on CF sample has intermediate sp2 and sp3 hybridization compared to CF and GQD samples. For GQDs grafted on CF sample, the deconvolution of O1s peaks (Fig. 3e), the peaks at 531.1eV (peak 1) and 533.6eV (peak 2) originates from oxygen doubly bonded to carbon (OC in quinones, ketones, and aldehydes) and oxygen singly bonded to carbon (O−C in ethers and phenols), respectively. Because oxygen atoms in carboxylic (−COOH) groups have both single and double bonds with carbon atoms, the oxygen atoms in −COOH groups can contribute to those two peaks [29,30]. In addition to the above mentioned peaks, the deconvolution of O1s peaks for GQDs grafted on CF sample produces an extra peak at 532.3eV (peak 3) which originates from carbonyl oxygen atoms in ester (C−O−CO) groups with 10.79 at.% oxygen, confirming the ester linkage between GQDs and CF [31].
XRD spectra of CF and GQD grafted on CF can be seen in SI (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of supercapacitive performances of CF and GQD-CF grafted structure
ce:section.ce:para: The supercapacitive performances of CF and GQD grafted on CF have been evaluated with a three−electrode cell configuration as well as in a two electrode capacitor device (Fig. 4 a). Fibres are pressed to attach with each other to form a woven cloth type flexible structure Fig. 4b shows the bent flexible supercapacitor with a bending angle of 120°. Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of the samples are measured with a potential window from 0.0V to +0.8V at a scan rate of 100mVs−1 in 1.0M H2SO4 aqueous electrolyte (Fig. 4c). In comparison to CF, the GQD-grafted CF exhibited high capacitive behaviour with increased areas of CV curves. The edge effect and the high surface area of the GQDs along with the increased surface defects on the CF surface after grafting with GQDs can lead to increased surface area as well as rapid ion diffusion. The specific capacitance calculated from CV curves acquired at 100mVs−1 is 211Fg−1 for GQDs grafted on CF and 40Fg−1 for CF which suggests that grafting GQDs on CF increases the capacitance by about 5 times over that of CF. Fig. 4d reveals the CV profile of GQDs grafted on CF at scan rates ranging from 20 to 100mVs−1. Consistent proportionate CV profiles with scan rates are observed that indicates good wetting and ease of access by ions [32]. To determine the specific capacitance by three electrode system, galvanostatic charge–discharge (C−D) measurements have been carried out at a current density of 1Ag−1 (Fig. 4e). The charge curves are nearly symmetric with respect to their corresponding discharge curves for the potential range tested. This indicates a high degree of reversibility between the C−D processes. The specific capacitance calculated from the discharge curves of GQD grafted on CF is 213Fg−1, which is approximately 5.5 fold compared to the value obtains for CF (39Fg−1). The galvanostatic C–D curves of GQDs grafted on CF at current densities of 1–10Ag−1 (Fig. 4f) present consistent profile shapes, indicating that the GQDs grafted on CF is stable over a wide range of current flows, and only about 1.25% of the specific capacitance is lost even at a high current density of 10Ag−1.

The findings of the current study indicate that the grafting of GQDs on CF generate enhanced electrode capacitance due to improved accessibility of electrolyte ions through GQDs active surface sites. The experimentally determined value of electrochemically active surface area of the GQDs on CF was 0.9cm2. Even though grafting by covalent bonding in general reduces the specific capacitance of electrochemical materials, however, the defects which are introduced on the CF surface due to GQD grafting providing the ability to accumulate more charges. Moreover, grafting by covalent bonding prohibits the GQD recombination. Both of these phenomena results in improvement in the capacitance.

To explore the application of GQDs-CF in flexible and wearable electronic devices, we have fabricated the textile electrodes using GQDs grafted on CFs as described in Section 2.6 (Fig. 5 a). To quantitatively investigate the electrochemical performance of the newly developed flexible supercapacitor, we have measured both charge–discharge and CV curves (Fig. S4). As can be seen in Fig. S4a, the triangular charge–discharge curves are directly associated with the electrical double layer, showing excellent capacitive behaviour with quick discharge rate. The capacitive behaviour in CV curves (Fig. S4b) at ultra-high scan rate (5Vs−1 and 10Vs−1) indicates the superior stability for practical applications. The shape of CV curves as well as V(t) dependence show deviation from ideal condition to some extent. The contact resistance was comparably higher than the typically used three electrode configuration where very small amount of active material is used (microgram scale).

The specific capacitance Cs defined as C/S (C is capacitance and S is total surface area of GQDs-CF within the cloth type electrodes) is calculated by using the slope of the discharge curves at all current densities (0.5–10) Ag−1 as depicted in Fig. S4c. A decreasing trend of capacitance with higher current rate is observed. However, only about 12% of the capacitance is lost even at 20 fold increase in current density from 0.5 to 10Ag−1. Furthermore to check the stability of the material under bending condition we have studied the electrochemical charge discharge properties while bending the electrode as shown in Fig. 5b. The results are presented in Fig. 5b along with unbent electrode; these clearly depict a minor drop in capacitance with good stability. This improved capacitance performance and stability can be attributed to the strong embedment of GQDs with CF, which provided better conductive surface as well as improved electron transfer network. Fig. 5c shows the percentage capacitance retention versus cycle number of the GQD-CF cloth flexible electrode. It can be seen from Fig. 5c that at 2Ag−1 current density, the cell was able to maintain approximately 97% of its initial value after 5000 cycles, suggesting a stable performance of the supercapacitor which can be suitable for fast charging-discharging applications.

A novel and effective low temperature chemical synthesis route is proposed to graft GQDs onto CF network. The homogeneous grafting of GQDs imparts electrochemical performance improvement with better stability. Also, this method is proved to be effective on prohibiting the recombination of GQDs. Apart from the flexible textile supercapacitors demonstrated in this study, the results show that this exploration would help to extend the application of GQDs in the field of biomedical imaging, photovoltaic devices, light-emitting diodes, environmental fields, fuel cells and non-destructive detection of nano-sized cracks in composite materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported under Australian Research Council's Discovery Projects funding scheme (project number DP 130103958). The Authors are thankful to ACMM and the Vibrational Spectroscopy Core Facility of the University of Sydney.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.mtcomm.2016.11.002.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

New method for the conversion of nitro groups into carbonyls
